prevalence.source: VIGITEL/DataSUS (estimated from 2022 data showing 22.4%, with upward trend)

incidence.value: Approximately 2-3% annual new cases among adults
incidence.source: Estimated from longitudinal studies like ELSA-Brasil

burden.source: IHME Global Burden of Disease Study 2021, adapted for Brazil

riskFactors: Unhealthy diet high in ultra-processed foods and sugars
Strong association with metabolic syndrome (including central obesity, insulin resistance, hypertension, dyslipidemia, and hyperglycemia)
vulnerablePopulations: Indigenous and Afro-Brazilian communities due to social determinants
epidemiology.diagnosticCriteria: Body Mass Index (BMI) ≥ 30 kg/m², per WHO classification; waist circumference for central obesity in metabolic syndrome context (>102 cm men, >88 cm women)

publicHealthSystem.screeningCoverage: About 60% in primary care settings via SUS, but lower in rural areas
publicHealthSystem.controlRate: Less than 15% achieve long-term weight management

regionalSpecificities.diagnosisCriteria: Aligned with WHO BMI standards, with emphasis on ethnic adaptations for Brazilian populations
regionalSpecificities.treatmentProtocol: SUS guidelines include nutritional counseling, physical activity programs, pharmacotherapy (e.g., GLP-1 agonists), and bariatric surgery for BMI ≥40 or ≥35 with comorbidities
regionalSpecificities.regionalChallenges: Socioeconomic inequalities leading to uneven access; high prevalence of ultra-processed food consumption in urban North and Northeast regions; integration with metabolic syndrome management strained by overburdened SUS
regionalSpecificities.strongPoints: National Nutrition Policy (NUPENS) and Family Health Strategy for community-based screening; growing telemedicine for remote areas
